You are free: to copy, distribute, display, and perform the work, and to make derivative works under the following conditions: you must attribute the work in the manner specified by the licensor; you may not use this work for commercial purposes; if you alter, transform, or build upon this work, you may distribute the resulting work only under a license identical to this one. For any reuse or distribution, you must make clear to others the license terms of this work. Any of these conditions can be waived if you get permission from the licensor. Your fair use and other rights are in no way affected by the above.

div.head: LECTURE XV.

GENESIS, XXVIII. 1.
And Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him, and said unto him, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan.
div.p: We cannot read the history of Abraham and his descendants, as related in this, the preceding, and some subsequent chapters of the book of Genesis, without being struck with the consistency and uniformity of advice, design, and conduct, on which the principal events seem to depend. It is in itself a very strong argument in favour of these Scriptures, as being genuine, and written under the direction of God. One plan evidently prevails through several generations and for many centuries. This is not the characteristic of human counsels; but of course must be the characteristic of divine decrees.

It is mentioned with great precision, as a circumstance of much consequence, that Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, connected themselves in marriage with those of thie rown family.
The consanguinity of Sarah, Rebekah, Leah, and Rachel, is detailed and dwelt on with a minuteness that could not fail of exciting our attention, even if no other peculiar circumstances had occured as tending to these several marraiges, or resulting from the. But in truth it is evident, that God did not disdain to lead by a special providence those whom he had chosen, through every stage of life. Both Isaac and Jacob were forbidden respectively by their parents, to take unto them wives from the surrounding nation: this interdiciton had the command of God united to that of a father; and the reason is plain—the surrounding nation in both instances were Canaanites. Now God had repeatedly declared that the Canaanite should be destroyed. It would, therefore, have been running counter to the declared will of God, had Abraham or had Isaac permitted their posterity to intermarry with an excommunicated tribe. It would have rendered the children of Israel very unwilling instruments of divine wrath, against those who had been so recently connected with them by the closest of human ties. On the contrary, so long as the Jews were confined to intermarriages among themselves, they were exactly adapted for all the great purposes which God had determined to effect by them as secondary agents. Therefore it was not unworthy of Moses to tell us,
that Isaac gave unto Jacob directions very similar to those which Abraham had given to his servant Eliezer, when he went to seek Rebekah for Isaac's future wife. Let us compare the two passages. Abraham spake to Eliezer thus: "I will make thee swear by the Lord the God of heaven, and the God of the earth, that thou shalt not take a wife unto my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, amongst whom I dwell; but thou shalt go unto my country and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son Isaac."Genesis 24:3 Here are two distinct but equally positive injunctions. Isaac is on no account to marry a daughter of Canaan, where he knew the true God was not known; and Abraham's most earnest desire is, that he should marry into his own family, where he felt sure the worship of the true God was yet preserved.

The extreme positiveness with which Abraham, at all events, forbids any marriage with a Canaanitess, even should his servant fail in the other part of his embassy, namely, that of prevailing with Rebekah, must not be passed over. It must be considered as a proof that Abraham felt assured of God's future vengeance on idolaters; to mix, therefore, with them, was not in his contemplation a possible alternative: yet at the same time he mentions his unalterable determination, that Isaac must remain in the land
of Canaan, for the Lord had sworn unto him, saying, "Unto thy seed will I give this land."Genesis 12:7 Now, what are the precise words of Isaac to Jacob, about a hundred years afterwards? "And Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him, and said unto him, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan: arise, go to Padan-aram, to the house of Bethuel, thy mother's father, and take thee a wife from thence of the daughters of Laban, thy mother's brother."Genesis 28:1-2 Here, again, we must remark, that Isaac's admonition, like that of old given by Abraham to Eliezer, contains two similar distinct, but equally positive directions. On no account is Jacob to marry a Canaanitess; but he is to marry one who stood to him in the same degree of relationship as existed between Isaac and Rebekah. So, then, after the lapse of a hundred years Isaac seems to have recognized the same rule of conduct as his father Abraham. At this distance of time we can receive no better historical evidence, that both considered themselves as acting in obedience to God, and in furtherance of his ulterior designs.

But the remainder of Isaac's address to his departing son, is very important. It unites the accuracy of a prophet with the fondness of a father. He proceeds to say, "And God Almighty bless thee, and make thee fruitful, and
multiply thee, that thou mayest be a multitude of people, and give thee the blessing of Abraham to thee and to thy seed with thee, that thou mayest inherit the land wherein thou art a stranger, which God gave unto Abraham."Genesis 28:3-4 How strong a proof here we, in this farewell benediction, that Isaac had a perfect faith in the precise promises of God as made to Abraham! He does not even think it necessary to mention tha these promises had been renewed to himself as they were at Gerar. No; he lays hold of the stronger stay; he falls back on a firmer rock: it was the promise made to Abraham. Thus did Isaac teach his son to lay up in his memory the real date of his birthright. Moreover, Isaac plainly predicts that Jacob should be the father of more heirs to the promise than himself or Abraham—"that thou mayest be a multitude of people."Genesis 28:3 This blessing was most strikingly fulfilled by the name of Israel being conferred on Jacob by God, and afterwards becoming the distinguishing name of his posterity as a nation.
